nativeRange.habitat: Outer lagoon and seaward reefs; commonly found sheltering in branching coral heads (notably Pocillopora eydouxi and Acropora colonies) at depths of about 1–50 m. Range extends from the Cocos-Keeling Islands east to Samoa and the Line Islands, north to southern Japan, and south to Lord Howe Island.

diet.notes: Accepts a varied diet of meaty foods (mysis, brine, finely chopped seafood), herbivore preparations, and quality flake or pellet foods. In the wild it has been documented feeding on zooplankton, copepods, and algae picked from the reef matrix around its host coral.

avoidWith: newly introduced damselfish in small tanks

imageCandidates.notes: Nick Hobgood (Nhobgood). 2484x1863. Crisp close-up of a group over Acropora clearly showing the two dark vertical bars on a silvery-yellow body with blue-tinged fins; best diagnostic and shoaling representation.
Nick Hobgood (Nhobgood). 2121x1592. Sharp wide shoal sheltering in branching Acropora, multiple individuals showing the two-bar pattern; illustrates natural coral-sheltering behaviour.
Bernard DUPONT. 2027x1216. Group over green Acropora reef; different setting for variety, several fish showing the two vertical bars.

summary: A small but pugnacious Indo-Pacific damselfish recognised by two dark vertical bars on a silvery-grey body and a blue-green tail; hardy and reef-safe but tends to become territorial as it matures.
careNotes: The Two Stripe Damselfish (Dascyllus reticulatus) goes by several other names in the trade — including Reticulate Dascyllus, Reticulated Damselfish, and Headband Humbug — and belongs to the same genus as the Four Stripe, Three Stripe, and Domino Damsels. It is generally considered an easy, hardy species and is often recommended to beginners, but its temperament tends to shift from manageable as a juvenile to notably aggressive as an adult. Adults have been known to harass similarly sized tankmates and to be territorial toward conspecifics, particularly new additions to the tank. For this reason it is generally best added late to a mixed community, or housed alongside other aggressive species that can hold their own. In the wild it typically shelters among branching corals, especially Pocillopora and Acropora colonies, so aquascaping with similar structure tends to bring out its natural behaviour. It is generally considered reef-safe — it does not harm corals or motile invertebrates — though it may bully smaller tankmates away from food and territory.
breedingNotes: The Two Stripe Damselfish reproduces by laying adhesive eggs directly on a surface — a strategy known as demersal spawning. Males prepare a nest site by mouth-cleaning a patch of rock or dead coral, the female then deposits her eggs on the cleared surface, and the male guards and aerates the clutch until the eggs hatch. Captive breeding has been reported but is uncommon in home aquaria.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source of retailer-style husbandry baseline: 30-gallon minimum, easy care, aggressive temperament, reef-safe, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, max 3.5 in.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacentridae), max size (9 cm TL), native range (Eastern Indian Ocean to Western Pacific, depth 1–50 m), habitat (outer lagoons and seaward reefs, associated with Pocillopora eydouxi), reproduction (oviparous, male nest cleaning, demersal eggs, paternal care), and habitat temperature range 24.6–28.9°C. FishBase records a warmer range than LiveAquaria; averaged to 23–28°C.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked common names (Reticulate dascyllus, two-stripe damselfish), max size (9 cm / 3.5 in), description (grey body with two vertical stripes and a blue-green tail), temperament (aggressive, harasses similarly sized fish), and aquarium hardiness.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked general damselfish husbandry parameters (temperature 73–81°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, omnivorous diet, reef-safe) used to corroborate LiveAquaria's species-specific figures.

fish.breedingNotes: Males select and mouth-clean a nest site on rock or dead coral, after which the female deposits adhesive eggs on the prepared surface. The male guards and aerates the clutch until hatching, and pair bonding tends to occur around the nest site. Captive spawning has been achieved but is rarely accomplished in typical home aquaria.
